title: Injecting Text Embeddings into the U-Net
concept: Text embeddings are fed into the diffusion U-Net at multiple layers, influencing the denoising process at every resolution level.

exercises.prompt: When you type a prompt like 'a cat wearing a hat', how do you think the AI actually 'reads' your words while drawing the image?

options.text: It turns the words into number lists (embeddings) and feeds them into the image-making network
It searches the internet for a matching photo
It ignores the words and just draws randomly

exercises.explain: Exactly! Your text gets converted into number lists called embeddings, and those numbers guide the AI's drawing process step by step.

cards.title: Whispers in the Ear of the Artist
cards.body: Imagine an artist painting with their eyes closed, but a helper keeps whispering hints like 'more red here' or 'add fluffy fur there' at every single stage of the painting. That's basically what happens inside a diffusion model! The text embedding keeps whispering guidance into the U-Net at every layer, not just once.

cards.title: What Does 'Injecting' Mean Here?
cards.body: Injecting text embeddings means the AI inserts the number version of your prompt directly into many layers of the U-Net (the network that cleans up noisy images). This happens at multiple resolutions — from blurry big shapes to tiny sharp details — so your words shape the picture at every single step.

cards.title: A Movie Director on Set
cards.body: Think of the U-Net as a film crew building a scene layer by layer — the set, the lighting, the costumes, the final touches. Your text prompt is like a director who doesn't just give instructions once at the start, but pops up at EVERY stage of filming to say 'remember, it's a sunset scene!' That constant reminder is the text embedding being injected repeatedly.

cards.title: From Blob to Butterfly
cards.body: Prompt: 'a blue butterfly on a flower.' At the U-Net's blurriest early layer, the text embedding nudges random noise toward a butterfly-like blob shape. At a middle layer, it nudges colors toward blue. At the final, most detailed layer, it nudges tiny edges to look like wing patterns and flower petals. The same text embedding guides every layer, just at different zoom levels.

cards.title: Myth: Text is Only Used at the Start
cards.body: Many beginners assume the AI reads your prompt once, then works alone. Not true! The text embedding is injected again and again, at nearly every layer of the U-Net, from the coarsest to the finest resolution. This constant guidance is why the final image still matches your words so closely.

cards.title: One-Time Hint vs. Constant Guidance
cards.body: Compare how a text prompt could theoretically be used versus how it's actually used in real diffusion models like Stable Diffusion.

left.text: AI reads the prompt once at the very start, then denoises the image completely on its own with no more guidance.

right.text: The text embedding is injected at multiple U-Net layers and resolutions, steering shapes, colors, and details throughout the whole denoising process.

exercises.prompt: Where in the U-Net are text embeddings typically injected?

options.text: Only at the very last layer, right before the image is finished
At multiple layers and resolution levels throughout the network
Only outside the U-Net, never inside it
Only in the first layer, then never again

exercises.explain: Text embeddings are injected repeatedly at many layers and resolutions, guiding the denoising process from coarse shapes to fine details.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Injecting text embeddings at multiple layers helps the AI shape both big features and tiny details according to the prompt.
exercises.explain: True! Early layers (low resolution) get guidance for overall shape, and later layers (high resolution) get guidance for fine details — all from the same text embedding.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence.
exercises.sentence: Text embeddings are injected into the U-Net at ____ layers so the prompt can guide the image at every resolution level.
exercises.explain: 'Multiple' is correct because the text embedding influences the denoising process throughout the whole network, not just once.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'Since the U-Net gets the text embedding once at the beginning, the rest of the image is generated with no connection to the prompt.' Is this statement AI-accurate?

options.text: No — text embeddings are injected repeatedly at many layers throughout the U-Net, not just once at the start
Yes — this is exactly how diffusion models work
Yes, but only for black and white images

exercises.explain: The statement is inaccurate. Real diffusion models inject the text embedding at multiple points across the U-Net, ensuring the prompt keeps guiding the image the whole way through.

exercises.prompt: Boss Challenge: A diffusion model is generating an image from the prompt 'a red dragon flying over mountains.' Which statement best describes how the text embedding influences the U-Net?

options.text: It is injected only once, at the lowest resolution layer, and never used again
It is injected at multiple layers across different resolutions, shaping both the overall dragon-and-mountain layout and the fine details like scales and clouds
It is used only after the image is fully generated, to check if the image matches the prompt
It replaces the noisy image entirely at the start of the process
It has no effect on the U-Net at all and only labels the final image

exercises.explain: Correct! The text embedding is repeatedly injected across many U-Net layers and resolutions, shaping the big picture (dragon shape, mountain layout) early on and fine details (scales, texture, clouds) later — that's how the whole image stays true to the prompt.
